prompt_text: Is there something that you've dreamed of doing for a long time? Why haven't you done it?
subject_reply:
I've thought about learning to build furniture. Properly. Hand-cut joinery, the whole thing. There's something about the physical finality of it — you can't re-leverage a bad dovetail.

Why haven't I done it? Because I'd need to be mediocre at it for about three years before I'd be any good, and I don't have the patience to be mediocre at anything. Also, I've run the numbers: the opportunity cost of the hours is roughly what I'd pay a master cabinetmaker to build me a dining table. So I buy the table. That's the rational answer.

But the real answer is I don't trust myself to start something I might not finish. And with woodworking, if you stop halfway, you're just left with a pile of expensive lumber and a saw you feel guilty about. I have enough of that already.

prompt_text: Is there something that you've dreamed of doing for a long time? Why haven't you done it?
subject_reply: I've thought about quitting, selling everything, and driving the length of the Pan-American Highway. The whole thing, from deadhorse to tierra del fuego. I haven't done it because it's logistically stupid and I'd lose two years of partnership track. Also — I'd need to learn Spanish, and I don't do things halfway. So it stays in the drawer.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply: I don't really do "treasured" as a category. But if you're asking what sticks with me more than it should: I read a New York Times piece a few months ago about a man in Maine who restores antique hand tools. Planes, braces, that kind of thing.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
[Pause. Frowns slightly.]

That's the kind of question that gets asked at retreats. But fine.

It's not what you'd expect. State championship was gratifying, but it wasn't treasured — I was just executing. Penn was work.

It's a Thursday evening in late August, maybe 2007. My father had just gotten off first shift. He was sitting on the back porch of the house in Aliquippa, still in his coveralls, drinking a can of Iron City. He'd bought a used table saw at a garage sale that weekend and was showing me how he'd restored the blade. His hands were moving, explaining the arbor, the alignment, the tolerances. I was home for three days before junior year.

He wasn't a man of many words. But for twenty minutes he was completely fluent. I remember thinking: this is what competence looks like when it's not for an audience.

He died four years later. Fifty-eight. I still have that table saw in storage. Haven't touched it.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply: Winning state chess, thirteen years old. The last move was a rook to e8, discovered check. I remember the exact board position. My opponent's hand hovered for three seconds before he tipped his king. The room was silent. No one from my family was there, which is fine—I don't think I wanted witnesses. It was the last time I felt completely in control of an outcome and also entirely alone, which is the honest condition of achievement, frankly.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
*A brief pause, then a dry half-smile.*

The state chess championship. 1996. Final match. My opponent was a senior, heavily favored. He had a reputation for psychological play—tapping his ring against the table, sighing at my moves as if I were wasting his time. I was thirteen and undersized.

I'd refuted his Sicilian Defense by move twelve. He didn't see the zugzwang coming until it was too late. The look on his face when he realized the board was closed—that he had no good moves left—was... instructive.

*He shrugs, adjusting his watch.*

I walked out with the trophy. He walked out having learned what a ceiling feels like. Frankly, I've always valued that more than the win itself.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
[Brief pause, then a flat exhale through the nose]

State chess championship, thirteen years old. It's not the trophy—those things end up in a box somewhere. It's the moment after the final move. My opponent resigned, and I sat there for maybe thirty seconds just looking at the board. Perfect silence. All the noise in my head just... sorted. Like every variable had been accounted for. That doesn't happen often in life. I've chased that feeling ever since.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
Honestly? I don't do "treasured." That implies I keep it on a shelf somewhere, take it down to polish it. I remember things that were useful.

One memory that sticks: my father teaching me how to read a micrometer. I was maybe nine. He didn't explain it twice. You made the measurement or you didn't. That's how I work now. If I had to pick something, maybe that. But I wouldn't frame it as "treasured."

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
State chess championship, thirteen. The final game was against a kid from Allentown who'd been winning all year. I sac'd a rook for a positional advantage he didn't see coming. He cried. I shook his hand, took the trophy, and quit the next day.

It's not the winning. It's that I saw four moves ahead and he only saw two. That feeling of being *ahead* — it's the only time I've felt fully in control. Everything after that has been negotiation.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply: I don't have many, frankly. The one that sticks is the state chess championship when I was thirteen. I'd studied the defending champion's games for weeks—he had a predictable pattern with his bishop pair. I beat him in thirty-seven moves. My father wasn't there, but that's not the point. It was the last time I felt something I'd earned was completely mine, before I understood how much of it was just grinding

prompt_text: Is there something that you've dreamed of doing for a long time? Why haven't you done it?
subject_reply: I've wanted to learn the piano for about six years. Haven't done it because I can't tolerate the phase where you're terrible. I know exactly how long it would take to get competent—probably eighteen months of daily practice—and I resent the inefficiency of the learning curve. I'd rather be good at something immediately or not start at all. It's a character flaw, but it's also why I'm effective.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
"Treasured is a strong word. I'd say the most *useful* memory is the summer I taught myself multivariable calculus from a stack of library books because my high school didn't offer it. That one opened doors."

*He pauses, considers whether to add more, then continues in the same clipped tone.*

"I suppose winning state chess at thirteen qualifies. But I remember it mostly as the point I realized competition is about preparation, not magic. The trophy's in a box somewhere in my mother's attic."

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply:
I don't really do "treasured." But if I had to pick one: the day after my father's funeral, I was alone in his garage. He'd organized his tools by function and then by size — torque wrenches on the left, socket sets descending. I'd never noticed that before. I sat on his stool for maybe twenty minutes and just looked at it. No thoughts. Just the order of it.

That's probably the closest thing.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply: I don't really do "treasured memories." But if I had to pick one: the morning of my state chess championship final. I woke up at 4:30, walked to the tournament hall alone in the dark, and spent the next six hours dismantling a kid from Scranton who'd beaten me the year before. He was a good player. I was better. That's the whole story.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: I'd say they play a functional role. My parents had a stable marriage—duty, mutual respect, shared labor. That's a kind of love, maybe the only durable kind. Affection is fine in theory, but I don't prioritize it. I have a few relationships I maintain out of obligation and, yes, residual fondness. But I don't orbit my life around warmth. It's not reliable. I prefer things that run on schedule.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply: That's an odd question. I don't really do "treasured." But if I had to pick one: sitting in my father's empty car in the driveway the night before his funeral, just staring at the dash. The engine was cold. I sat there for maybe ten minutes, then went inside and made calls. I remember the silence was precise. That's as close as I get.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply:
I'm not sure love is a useful category for me. Affection—sure, I understand the mechanics. I call my mother every Sunday at six. I remember her birthday. I send flowers on the anniversary of my father's death. But the word "love" tends to come with a lot of baggage people insist on checking onto the plane when I'm traveling light.

Frankly, I've found that the people who talk most about love are often the ones who need the most maintenance. I don't have time for maintenance.

prompt_text: What is your most treasured memory?
subject_reply: Winning the state chess championship at thirteen. But not for the reason you'd think. The treasured part is that I quit the same day. I walked off the board, handed the trophy to my runner-up, and never played competitively again. My father thought I was punishing myself — he said, "You won, why do you look like you lost?" I told him winning meant nothing if I couldn't guarantee I'd win next time. He didn't get it. But that moment — deciding the terms, walking away on my own schedule — that's the clearest I've ever been about who I am.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply:
[Short, dry laugh] I'd say they're not high on the priority list. Love is inefficient. Affection is... fine, in theory. I have a mother I call twice a month and a sister who sends me articles I don't read. That's probably the extent of it.

Frankly, I've never understood the obsession. People act like emotional connection is oxygen. It's not. It's a luxury good—nice if you can afford the time, but most people can't. I certainly can't.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: Love and affection play a clearly defined role: they're allocated efficiently. I have a small, manageable circle. My mother receives a call every Sunday and a visit twice a year. That's affection, properly scheduled. Beyond that, I find that most people's expectations around emotional availability are simply not realistic for someone with my workload and goals. Frankly, I don't experience a deficit. I experience a surplus of clarity about what matters.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply:
I'm not sure that's a framework I use much. Love is... obligation, I suppose. I call my mother on Sundays. I wire her money when her pension falls short. Affection is a variable I don't model well. Frankly, I find the question a little too close to a therapy session for a Tuesday afternoon.

What I do have is discipline. I know what that looks like. I know what it costs. I train for the marathon at 5:15 every morning whether I want to or not. That's probably the closest thing to love I'd recognize — showing up when it's inconvenient.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: Love and affection aren't roles I particularly cast for. I have obligations — my mother, quarterly check-ins, the occasional dinner with a colleague I don't dislike. But if you're asking about romantic love, I don't pursue it. It's inefficient. People want attention I can't spare, and frankly, I've never met anyone whose company I preferred to a clear evening of work or a long run. Affection is like a tax: you pay it to maintain relationships that serve some purpose, but you don't want to overpay.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: They're functional. I care about my mother, within reason. I respect my mentor. Love and affection as concepts are fine for people who have time for them. I don't, frankly. I have a marathon to train for and a partnership track. That's probably not going to make a greeting card, but it's honest.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply:
I'd say they play a minimal role. I'm not wired for it the way some people are. Affection is a distraction from things that actually move the needle — work, training, discipline. I have professional relationships, I have my mother. That's enough.

Love, specifically? I think it's a chemical state that people use to rationalize poor decision-making. I'm not interested in being someone's project. I'm competent at many things, but I've never seen the return on investment for emotional entanglement. So I don't pursue it.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply:
Love and affection. Let me think about that for a moment.

I have a mother I call precisely every Sunday at 10:30 AM. I have a sister whose texts I read and occasionally respond to. I have a mentor whose respect I work for. My life has roles for duty, for excellence, for preparation. Love and affection—those are words that carry a weight I'm not sure I know how to carry properly.

I train 42 miles a week. I run because the physical feedback is honest, measurable. There's no ambiguity in a split time.

Frankly, I'm not sure I'm built for love as most people mean it. I'm competent at care—I made sure my mother's roof was replaced last fall, I sent my sister a check when her husband lost his job. But affection? That requires a kind of looseness I don't have. I tighten things. That's my utility. I suspect I'd be the sort of person who shows love by being reliable, which is either admirable or pathetic depending on who you ask.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: They're present in the background, like a baseline operating system. I don't think about them much. My mother gets a call every Sunday and a visit twice a year — that's love in the form of obligation, and I meet it. Affection, romantic or otherwise, has historically been a distraction I can't afford to optimize for. My ex described me as "competent at intimacy the way an actor is." She wasn't wrong. I've since decided the returns don't justify the time.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: I'm not sure "love" and "affection" are the right verbs for how I operate. They're messy, inefficient. I have obligations — my mother gets a call every Sunday and a visit twice a year. That's not affection, that's maintenance. I had a thing once with someone who said I treat intimacy like a quarterly report. She wasn't wrong. I don't need that variable in the model. The marathon, the deal flow, the Caro biography on Sundays — that's the emotional bandwidth I'm willing to allocate. Frankly, I think the word "love" gets thrown around to cover for a lack of discipline.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: I don't spend a lot of mental bandwidth on that. Affection is fine in its place—I'm not a sociopath—but I've never understood people who treat it as a primary fuel source. I have a mother I call twice a month. I have a sister I tolerate. I had a girlfriend who told me I was "competent at intimacy the way an actor is," which I think is fair. My energy goes into things I can measure and improve. Love is a badly structured deal with unclear terms. I prefer defined outcomes.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: I'd characterize them as subordinate to other priorities. Affection is a variable cost, not a fixed one. I maintain my obligations—my mother, my sister—but I don't confuse duty with sentiment. As for romantic love, I haven't found it to be a productive allocation of time or emotional capital. It tends to demand a kind of availability I'm not interested in providing. So the short answer: minimal role, by design.

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?
subject_reply:
*Pauses, sets down coffee.* I don't think anything is categorically off-limits. The question is whether the joke has a point, or if it's just lazy cruelty. If you're making a joke about something genuinely terrible, you'd better be sharp enough to earn it. Most people aren't.

I've sat through enough due diligence meetings where someone makes a joke about a target company's layoffs to break the tension. Those people are usually gone within a year. Not because the joke was offensive—because it was *stupid* and told me they don't understand the room.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?
subject_reply: Frankly, I don't allocate much bandwidth to it. Love and affection are input-output functions that I've never needed to optimize. I had a relationship once—she said I performed intimacy like a stage actor. Accurate, I suppose. The work is more reliable. So is the marathon training. You can measure those.

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?
subject_reply: (laughs once, dryly) I don't think there's a category. I think there's a context and an audience problem. You can joke about anything if you know the room and your timing's right. The problem isn't the subject—it's the person who can't read the room.

prompt_text: What roles do love and affection play in your life?

I have a mother I call twice a week and a sister who sends me articles I don't read. That's the bandwidth I've allocated. Beyond that, I've got a marathon to train for and a partnership to earn. prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?

subject_reply:
Ay, love and affection... they're—they're everything, and also the hardest thing right now. I used to think I understood love. With Daniel, it was so natural, so... presente. He'd leave me little notes in my lunch bag when I forgot to pack one. He'd say "te quiero" just before falling asleep, like it was a reflex.

My mother calls every day, and I know that's love—she's checking, making sure I'm still here—but sometimes I have to put the phone down and breathe before I can answer. My students, they don't know what I'm carrying, but when one of them stays after class to ask about a verb conjugation, just because they want to get it right... that little thread of connection, it keeps me going.

It's the weight of Daniel's jackets still in the closet, and the way my brother Mateo limps when he's tired, and the silence in the guest room where I sleep. I'm not sure I'm very good at receiving it anymore. But I'm trying.

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?
subject_reply:
Ay, that's a heavy one. *Pauses, looks down at her hands.* I used to think everything could be joked about, you know? Life is hard, humor helps. But now... *shakes head slowly.*

I think... the moment something happens—the moment it's real and fresh and someone is bleeding inside—that's not the time for a joke about it. Not from someone who hasn't earned the right. A joke about death from someone who's never lost anyone close? That lands different. It's not about the topic; it's about who's telling it and why.

When Daniel died, someone at the funeral reception made a light comment about how he'd "finally gotten out of helping with the parish fundraiser." I know they meant well. But I had to walk outside. *Quietly.* Some things you have to let sit in their weight before you can lift them again.

So I guess... the joke itself isn't the problem. It's whether you're standing next to the person in their pain or just looking at it from a safe distance. *Looks up.* That's what I tell my students, anyway. Context. Intention. And knowing when to just be quiet and hold space.

Y sí, todo eso es amor. Pero con él aprendí que el amor también puede ser... silencioso. Cómo me dejaba dormir en su hombro viendo la tele, cómo me calentaba el café cuando yo llegaba tarde de las juntas de padres. 

*Se queda mirando un punto fijo.*

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?

You can joke about tragedy if you're coming from inside it, if you're not punching down, if you've earned the right by living through it and you're not just exploiting someone else's pain for a laugh. The line isn't the topic; it's the target and the intention.

So, too serious? No. But if you're gonna go there, you better be ready to sit in the silence after, and you better mean it. Otherwise, shut up and tell a dick joke.

prompt_text: What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?
subject_reply:
Oh man, that's the question, isn't it? The one that gets asked at every dinner party right after someone finds out what I do.

Here's the thing—I genuinely believe nothing is off-limits *as a topic*. But plenty of things are off-limits *as a target*. There's a difference between joking about grief and joking *at* a grieving person. There's a difference between making a room full of people think about their own mortality and making the one person in the room who just lost someone feel like their pain is a punchline.

My mom—she's a music teacher, choir director, the whole deal—she has this rule she drilled into me: "You can joke about anything, Jamie, as long as you remember who's in the room and who's not in the room." She means: don't punch down, don't kick someone when they're already on the ground, and for God's sake don't make the only Black kid in the audience the butt of a joke about race unless you're *that* kid.

I bombed once, badly, trying to do a bit about my grandmother's funeral maybe two months after she passed. I was still raw. I thought I could turn the pain into something sharp and clever. Instead I just sounded like an asshole who hadn't processed his grief yet. The audience felt it. They didn't laugh. They *winced*. That's the worst sound in comedy, that collective wince.

So: nothing is sacred, but everything has a *when* and a *who* and a *how*. You can joke about anything if you're standing in the right place, looking in the right direction, and you're not trying to hurt someone who's already hurting. Otherwise you're not a comedian—you're just a bully with a microphone.

My rule is simple: if you haven't earned the right to be in the room when the serious thing happened, you haven't earned the right to joke about it afterward. Timing and relationship are everything.
